items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: What is the scope of a local variable?
items.correct: Accessible only within the function/block where it is declared.
items.random_wrong: Stored on a local computer rather than a server.
items.why: Students confuse 'local' with 'on this computer'. In programming, scope refers to the CODE REGION where the variable name resolves - local variables vanish when the function returns.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1959. “1945” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's LIFO. “FIFO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Dijkstra published his shortest path algorithm?
items.why: Exact answer: 1959 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
